initiative_name: 4.1. Action on Governorate buildings and services
parent_statement: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
coverage_assessment: Sub-actions collectively address the parent’s stated perimeter (Governorate buildings and services): municipal buildings (awareness + equipment upgrades), street lighting (fund + strategic plan), water/wastewater (efficiency assessment + SCADA consideration), solid waste (assessment + recycling/methane options + awareness), fleet (management + replacement), and cross-cutting awareness capacity (EAU). A key gap is the absence of defined performance targets (beyond one indicative % claim) and deadlines for each service area, which limits the ability to demonstrate leadership with trackable results.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary and logically grouped by major municipal service systems, with enabling actions (EAU, assessments, strategic planning, revolving fund) supporting implementation actions (LEDs/sensors, equipment replacement, fleet renewal). There is some redundancy between general awareness activities (Actions 1 and 8) and several actions remain at the assessment/plan stage without a clear sequence to implementation.
weak_signals: no explicit deadlines/years across all sub-actions, heavy reliance on assessments/strategic plans/awareness, few measurable outcome targets (only one indicative %), repeated “would gain/should/could” phrasing
strong_signals: clear institutional perimeter (Governorate/City responsibilities), concrete retrofit-style measures (LED lighting, motion sensors, HVAC management), revolving fund with reinvested savings concept, quantified efficiency potential claim (30% or more) for variable speed/high-efficiency pumps
initiative_reasoning: This initiative aims to reduce energy use and improve climate performance in Luxor’s own public buildings and municipal services (street lighting, water/wastewater, waste, fleet) to demonstrate leadership. Sub-action strength mix: 0 strong, 4 moderate (Actions 2, 3, 5, 7), and 4 weak (Actions 1, 4, 6, 8), largely because timelines and explicit outcome targets are mostly absent and several items are assessment/awareness focused. The parent provides strong inherited spatial/institutional scope (acting on the Governorate/City’s direct perimeter) but does not provide an inherited binding mechanism (no ordinance/mandate), so weaker subs are not lifted into enforceability. Coverage is high because all named service domains are addressed; coherence is good because actions form a plausible pathway (capacity/awareness + assessments/plans + investment mechanisms + concrete upgrades), though sequencing to implementation is often implicit. Applying the initiative soundness rule: it fails SOUND because aggregate_measurability is 'No' and there is no 'strong' sub-action; it qualifies as PARTIAL because coverage_score >= 0.4, multiple sub-actions are concrete/moderate, and there is at least one measurable element (the 30%+ efficiency reduction claim) even without deadlines.
parent_row_type_reasoning: The parent text is not merely a section label; it states a substantive priority commitment that the Governorate/City Council should act on its direct responsibilities (buildings and services) to be exemplary and mobilize stakeholders.

initiative_name: 4.2. Action plan on the City of Luxor
parent_statement: Luxor will implement the action plan on the City of Luxor by mobilizing stakeholders (residents, shops, businesses, tourism operators, farmers, etc.) to achieve energy reduction and renewable energy development.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively address the parent goal of mobilizing stakeholders for energy reduction and renewable energy via transport, tourism, residential, tertiary, and industry interventions (plans, funds/units, audits, retrofits, solar thermal/PV promotion). However, the parent aspires to “significant reduction” and “development of renewable energy” without any initiative-wide quantified targets; the subs also do not specify scale (how many buildings/boats/hotels) or required participation levels, leaving a major gap for tracking progress.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary and sector-organized: transport planning/coordination, tourism support and vessel electrification, buildings awareness + solar hot water + retrofits, and industrial audits + a support unit. There is some redundancy across multiple “awareness/mapping/technical support” items, but no contradictions or conflicting directions are evident.
weak_signals: no explicit deadlines/years, no quantified outcome targets (MW/%, units) across subs, promote/ideally/could language, heavy reliance on awareness/mapping/technical support
strong_signals: implementation structures (master plan, public transport authority, support units), creation of funds (tourism/boats), concrete interventions (RTB airport-to-centre, solar PV on boats/docks, solar water heating, retrofit financing via bank), GHG emissions measurement methodology
initiative_reasoning: The initiative seeks stakeholder-wide energy consumption reduction and renewable energy development within Luxor’s boundaries, spanning transport, tourism, buildings, and industry. Sub-action strength mix: 0 strong, 6 moderate (Actions 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 10), and 4 weak (Actions 1, 5, 8, 9), with weaknesses driven mainly by missing timelines and quantifiable outcome targets. The parent provides clear spatial/institutional scope (“within its boundaries” and mobilizing stakeholders) but does not establish a binding mechanism (no ordinance/mandate), so it cannot convert the largely promotional language into enforceable commitments. Coverage is fairly broad across major sectors (good coverage_score), and coherence is high because the measures form a plausible delivery chain (plans/units/funds + technical support + some concrete investments). However, aggregate measurability is not met because the cluster lacks explicit targets and deadlines needed to track progress, so it fails the SOUND rule (no ‘strong’ subs and aggregate_measurability = No) but qualifies as PARTIAL due to multiple concrete programmatic actions and at least some measurable element mentioned in-text (indicative savings at 24°C) even though not framed as a target.
parent_row_type_reasoning: The parent text states a substantive commitment/need to “mobilize all stakeholders acting in its boundaries” to achieve “reduction in energy consumption and development of renewable energy,” which functions as an initiative-level policy aim rather than a purely structural section label.

initiative_name: Section V: Project Fiches — Priority Action #1 for SECAP
parent_statement: Priority Action #1: Urban Sustainable Mobility Master Plan (SCGUMMP) for Luxor to improve mobility while reducing energy consumption and GHG emissions from transport, with a project lifetime of 1 year (Oct 2017–Dec 2018).
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively cover the parent goal of improving mobility while reducing energy use and GHGs: they produce a 15-year mobility plan with costing/roadmap (Action 1), establish emissions/benefits measurement and reporting (Action 2), and define a quantified 2030 energy-reduction impact tied to implementation of the plan (Action 3). A gap is that concrete implementation instruments (e.g., specific projects, regulatory changes, or budget commitments) are not detailed here beyond the plan and its intended impacts.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary and logically sequenced: Action 1 defines the plan and implementation roadmap, Action 2 defines how to measure and report impacts, and Action 3 states the expected implementation milestone and 2030 energy-reduction outcomes. No internal contradictions are apparent, though the project lifetime (ending 2018) contrasts with Action 3’s statement that implementation would have started by 2020 at the latest (suggesting the fiche is for planning rather than delivery).
weak_signals: No explicit binding/enforcement mechanism, MRV action lacks deadline, implementation specifics/projects not enumerated (beyond plan and expected impacts)
strong_signals: City of Luxor scope, project dates Oct 2017–Dec 2018, 15-year master plan deliverable, 30% energy reduction by 2030, implementation started by 2020 latest, 50% progress by 2030, quantified measure contributions (10%+5%+10%+5%)
initiative_reasoning: 1) The initiative aims to create and operationalize a sustainable urban mobility master plan for Luxor and track/realize associated transport energy/GHG reductions. 2) Strength mix: 2 strong sub-actions (Actions 1 and 3) and 1 moderate sub-action (Action 2). 3) The parent clearly supplies spatial scope (City of Luxor) and a defined project window (Oct 2017–Dec 2018), but it does not indicate an enforceable mechanism (ordinance/mandate), so weaker elements are not lifted by binding authority. 4) Coverage is strong because planning deliverables, MRV, and a quantified 2030 impact target are all present; remaining gaps relate to unspecified on-the-ground projects and policy instruments. Coherence is strong because the components form a sensible chain from plan → monitoring → impact milestones/targets, with only a minor timing nuance between the 2018 project end and later implementation expectations. 5) SOUNDNESS RULE: coverage_score (0.75) >= 0.6, coherence_score (0.8) >= 0.5, aggregate_measurability = Yes, all sub-actions are strong/moderate (>= half), and at least one is strong (in fact two). Therefore initiative_result = SOUND and final_verdict applies given confidence >= 0.55.

The solar PV item is only a study (not deployment), so renewable electricity supply for onboard loads is not fully covered as an implemented measure within the stated project lifetime.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary: (1) fleet fuel conversion delivers direct fuel switching, (3) fleet management targets efficiency gains, and (2) a PV study can support a later electrification/renewables step. There is no internal contradiction, though the PV item is less mature (study-only) compared with the other more action-oriented elements.

initiative_reasoning: This initiative aims to decarbonize Luxor’s tourist boating sector by shifting boats from diesel to LPG/CNG and improving fleet operations, with an overall project window of Dec 2017–Dec 2020 stated in the parent text. Strength mix: 0 strong, 2 moderate (boat conversion counts; 30% efficiency improvement), 1 weak (PV is study-only with no quantified outcome or timeline). The parent provides a clear temporal frame (start/end) and a clear technical direction (conversion away from fossil fuel/diesel to LPG/CNG), but it does not establish an explicit binding mechanism (e.g., ordinance/mandate) or explicit spatial scope keywords like “citywide,” so those are marked No. Coverage is strong because the numbered conversion and efficiency goal directly implement the parent intent; the PV study is additive but not essential for coverage. Coherence is high because the actions logically fit together (fuel switch + operational efficiency + enabling study). coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary and non-redundant: Action 1 reduces electricity demand, while Actions 2 and 3 add renewable electricity and renewable thermal energy. The parent provides a clear institutional/spatial scope (Governorate/municipal buildings in Luxor), but it does not establish an enforceable binding mechanism (e.g., ordinance/mandate), so subs are treated as programmatic commitments rather than regulatory requirements. initiative_name: City of Luxor (Governorate of Luxor) - Priority Action # 4 for SECAP
parent_statement: Priority Action #4: Sustainable and Green Tourism Plan (SGTP) to green hotels and resorts by promoting energy conservation/efficiency and renewable solutions (solar PV and solar water heating), starting Dec 2017 for 5 years.

However, the parent also mentions broader energy conservation/efficiency and renewable solutions generally, which are not fully detailed beyond the financing mechanism and the gas supply action.
coherence_assessment: The two sub-actions are complementary: one creates financing capacity for solar-related upgrades while the other delivers an infrastructure/fuel-switch measure with quantified impacts. No contradictions are apparent, but the measures are somewhat narrow relative to the broader SGTP framing (efficiency + renewables across hotel energy needs).
weak_signals: No explicit binding/regulatory mechanism, Limited detail on energy efficiency actions beyond general promotion, One sub-action lacks an explicit deadline/year, Emissions reduction figure appears potentially inconsistent with energy savings (as stated)
strong_signals: Start date December 2017, Project lifetime 5 years, Phases of 10 hotels per year, €500k/year investment capacity for first two years, 8,157 MWh/year electricity savings (8 GWh/year), Quantified GHG reduction (2.8 tCO2eq/y as stated), Defined scope (hotels/resorts; eastern bank)
initiative_reasoning: This initiative aims to green Luxor’s hotels and resorts under a Sustainable and Green Tourism Plan by promoting energy conservation/efficiency and deploying renewable/cleaner energy solutions, with a stated start (Dec 2017) and 5-year lifetime. Sub-action strength mix: 1 strong (revolving fund with phased deployment rate and near-term timeline) and 1 moderate (natural gas supply with quantified savings/emissions but no explicit deadline). The parent provides clear spatial/institutional scope (hotels/resorts; Luxor; eastern bank for the gas measure) but does not provide an explicit binding mechanism (e.g., ordinance/mandate). Coverage is good (0.7) because the subs directly support greening through finance + energy supply changes, though they do not fully specify the broader efficiency/renewables program implied by the parent. Coherence is high (0.8) because the actions are complementary and non-conflicting, forming a plausible pathway (enable investments + implement a concrete infrastructure intervention). Applying the initiative soundness rule: coverage_score >= 0.6, coherence_score >= 0.5, aggregate_measurability is Yes, both subs are strong/moderate (>= half), and at least one is strong—therefore the initiative is SOUND.
